# Chapter 05 - Chapter 5

## Draft Paragraphs (keyed)

[[CH05-P0010]] The transport hummed to a stop at the salve district platform, and Marnette didn't move. Through the grimy window, she could see the familiar gray corridors of her home sector, the flat artificial lighting that had once felt normal but now seemed harsh after weeks in Frost's sun-drenched chambers. People moved past the transport—salves in gray tunics heading to work shifts, children in smaller versions of the same uniform walking toward the education center, elderly residents shuffling toward the community gardens. Normal life. Her life, before.

[[CH05-P0020]] "Salve District Seven, final stop," the transport announced. "All passengers must disembark."

[[CH05-P0030]] Marnette gathered her small bag and stepped onto the platform. The air smelled different here—recycled and flat, lacking the subtle hints of old books and expensive antiseptic that had permeated Frost's residence. The red dust that coated everything in the colony seemed thicker here, grittier, settling into the seams of her tunic and the creases of her boots. She had been gone for twenty-three days. It felt like years.

[[CH05-P0040]] The walk to her quarters took her through corridors she had traveled thousands of times—past the communal dining hall with its long tables and institutional lighting, past the recreation area where children played games she had played herself as a child, past the memorial wall where names of the dead were inscribed in neat rows, each one a life lived and ended according to Elom's design. Her grandmother's name was there somewhere, among hundreds of others. Fifty-three years. A good long life. For a salve.

[[CH05-P0050]] Her quarters were exactly as she had left them—small, functional, clean. The sleeping pad in the corner, the narrow closet with her three identical gray tunics, the tiny window that looked out at nothing but the corridor wall of the adjacent building. On the bedside table, her mother's carving of Elom waited where she had left her copy when she packed for the assignment. She had taken the original with her to Frost's residence. Now both sat side by side, twin reminders of a faith that had felt unshakeable just weeks ago.

[[CH05-P0060]] She unpacked slowly, putting her belongings back in their proper places. The device Frost had given her that first night—the one with the early colony records—she had left behind. Too dangerous to bring into the salve district, too incriminating to be found with. But the words from those records were already burned into her memory: genetic markers, biological castes, population control through genetic programming. And the phrase from the book in his Archive: Project Mayfly. Optimized 50-year lifecycle parameters.

[[CH05-P0070]] A knock at the door startled her. She opened it to find Lena standing in the corridor, her hands still stained green from the hydroponics nutrient solutions, her face breaking into a wide smile that crinkled the corners of her eyes.

[[CH05-P0080]] "You're back!" Lena pulled her into a fierce hug, the familiar scent of growing things clinging to her clothes. "When did you arrive? Why didn't you send word? I would have met you at the platform."

[[CH05-P0090]] "Just now." Marnette returned the embrace, trying to feel the warmth of it, trying to let it anchor her back into the life she had known. "The assignment ended... suddenly."

[[CH05-P0100]] Lena pulled back, studying her face with the concerned attention of a longtime friend. Her eyes, brown and warm and utterly trusting, searched Marnette's expression for something she couldn't quite articulate. "I heard. Everyone's heard. Master Frost died three days ago. The announcement came through all the public channels—year-long state funeral, colony-wide mourning period, the whole protocol." She squeezed Marnette's hands. "Are you alright? You look... different."

[[CH05-P0110]] "Tired," Marnette said. "It was a difficult assignment."

[[CH05-P0120]] "Of course it was. Caring for a dying Master—I can't even imagine. Seven hundred ninety-three years old. Can you even comprehend that? All those centuries of memory, all those generations he watched come and go." Lena guided her toward the bed, sitting down beside her on the thin mattress. "Tell me about it. Or don't, if you'd rather not. Whatever you need."

[[CH05-P0130]] Marnette looked at her friend's open, honest face, at the familiar green stains on her fingers, at the prayer beads visible beneath her collar. Lena was faithful, genuinely faithful, the kind of person who found comfort in Elom's teachings and never questioned the order of things. She had been Marnette's closest friend since childhood, had stood beside her at her grandmother's funeral, had celebrated with her when she completed her caregiver training. How could Marnette explain what she had experienced? How could she describe the fragments of Frost's delirium, the photographs of Elena living to eighty-seven, the terrible possibility that everything they had been taught was built on deliberate lies?

[[CH05-P0140]] "He said some strange things," Marnette finally managed. "Before the end. Dying people often do."

[[CH05-P0150]] "Strange how?"

[[CH05-P0160]] "Just... confused. Feverish ramblings about the past, about decisions made centuries ago." Marnette forced a smile that felt like wearing a mask. "Nothing that made sense. You know how it is with the dying—their minds wander to places we can't follow."

[[CH05-P0170]] Lena nodded, her expression sympathetic. "That's what happened with my grandmother too. She kept talking to people who weren't there, remembering things that never happened. Once she was convinced she was back on Earth, describing oceans and forests as if she had seen them herself." She paused, her expression softening. "But you're home now. That's what matters. You can rest, recover, get back to normal."

[[CH05-P0180]] Normal. The word felt like a foreign language. What was normal now? How could she return to a life built on foundations she no longer trusted?

[[CH05-P0190]] "There's a service tonight," Lena continued, unaware of the turmoil beneath Marnette's calm surface. "Priest Malachi is speaking about Master Frost's death, about what it means for the colony. The whole district will be there. You should come. It might help."

[[CH05-P0200]] "Yes," Marnette said, though her stomach tightened at the thought. "I should come."

[[CH05-P0210]] \*\*\*

[[CH05-P0220]] The Church of Elom was fuller than Marnette had ever seen it. Word of a Master's death had drawn salves from across the district, filling the modest space until people stood along the walls and crowded into the corridor outside. The mosaics depicting the colony's founding seemed to glow in the enhanced lighting—Elon Musk gazing at the red planet, the first habitats rising from Martian soil, the first salves being born into their designated roles.

[[CH05-P0230]] Elon Musk. Not Elom. The names that had always seemed so different now seemed terrifyingly similar, separated by nothing more than a few letters and centuries of deliberate obfuscation.

[[CH05-P0240]] Marnette found a place near the back, beside Lena, letting the familiar atmosphere wash over her. The smell of candle wax and recycled air, the murmur of prayers being whispered, the rustle of gray tunics as people settled into their seats. The church's curved ceiling rose toward the dome's interior, humble but beautiful in its simplicity. No gold leaf here, no expensive tapestries—everything was functional, meaningful, designed to inspire rather than impress. This had always been her sanctuary, the place where the chaos of the world resolved into meaningful order. Now it felt like standing in a theater, watching actors perform a play whose script she had glimpsed backstage.

[[CH05-P0250]] She recognized faces in the crowd—fellow caregivers she had trained with, neighbors from her corridor, elderly salves she had cared for in years past. They all wore the same expression of reverent anticipation, the same unquestioning faith that she had worn herself just weeks ago. How strange to be among them now, looking the same on the outside while feeling utterly different within.

[[CH05-P0260]] Priest Malachi approached the altar, his white robes stark against the simple wood, his silver hair catching the light. At fifty-seven, he was one of the oldest salves in the district, his face lined with decades of faithful service. His voice had always seemed to Marnette like the voice of Elom himself—calm, certain, reassuring. When he raised his hands, the congregation fell silent.

[[CH05-P0270]] "Brothers and sisters in Elom," he began, his voice carrying through the packed space. "We gather tonight in a moment of profound significance. Master Timon Frost, who lived among us for seven hundred ninety-three years, has completed his journey. His death marks the end of an era—he was one of the last who remembered the founding, one of the last who had walked with those who knew Elom himself."

[[CH05-P0280]] Marnette's hands clenched in her lap. Elom himself. As if Elom had been a person who could be known, who could be walked with, who could be remembered. Which, if Frost was right, he had been. A man named Elon, transformed by time and deliberate revision into something divine.

[[CH05-P0290]] "Let us pray," Priest Malachi said, and the congregation bowed their heads.

[[CH05-P0300]] Marnette closed her eyes, her lips moving automatically with the familiar words. "Elom, creator and guide, who designed us for purpose, who gave each of us our role in the great colony, who sees all and knows all, help us to serve with devotion, to accept with grace, to find joy in our designated time."

[[CH05-P0310]] But the words stuck in her throat, each phrase now carrying echoes she couldn't unhear. Designed us for purpose—or engineered us for function? Gave each of us our role—or assigned each of us our caste? Designated time—or predetermined termination?

[[CH05-P0320]] She opened her eyes and found she couldn't close them again. Around her, the congregation prayed with sincere devotion, their faces peaceful, their faith untroubled. Lena's lips moved silently beside her, her expression serene. They didn't know. They couldn't know. And part of her envied them that ignorance, that ability to find genuine comfort in words that had become poison to her.

[[CH05-P0330]] Priest Malachi's sermon focused on the meaning of Master deaths, on the rare privilege of witnessing such events, on the year-long mourning that demonstrated the colony's gratitude for Master guidance. He spoke of Frost's contributions to historical scholarship, his centuries of service on various colony councils, his reputation for fairness and wisdom.

[[CH05-P0340]] "Master Frost dedicated his long life to understanding our past," Priest Malachi continued, his voice rich with reverence. "His Archive contained records from the earliest days of the colony, documents that helped us understand the vision of our founder. Through his scholarship, we learned to appreciate the wisdom of Elom's design, the perfection of the system that sustains us."

[[CH05-P0350]] He didn't mention the vote. Seven hundred twelve to three hundred eighty-nine. He didn't mention Project Mayfly or optimized lifecycle parameters. He didn't mention that the man being mourned had spent his final weeks trying to undo a small part of what he had helped create.

[[CH05-P0360]] "Master Frost's death reminds us of our own mortality," Priest Malachi continued. "Not with fear, but with purpose. Our lives may be brief compared to theirs, but that brevity is itself a gift. We are focused, essential, undiluted by centuries of accumulated regret. We serve, we contribute, we pass on our knowledge to the next generation, and then we return to Elom's embrace. This is the sacred cycle that sustains our colony, that has sustained it since the founding."

[[CH05-P0370]] Sacred cycle. Marnette thought of the scan results she had seen during Frost's crisis—cells actively terminating themselves, programmed death triggered throughout his body. Was that what awaited every salve at fifty or sixty? Not natural aging, but deliberate shutdown? A kill switch embedded in their very genetics?

[[CH05-P0380]] The congregation murmured agreement around her. "Blessed be the cycle," voices said in unison, and Marnette's lips moved with the words though no sound emerged.

[[CH05-P0390]] "Some look at the Masters and see only longevity," Priest Malachi said, echoing the sermon she had heard weeks ago in this same church. "They see hundreds of years and think: that is blessing. But Elom teaches us that blessing is not measured in time, but in impact, in purpose, in devotion. A salve who lives fifty years but serves faithfully, who raises children in the faith, who contributes to the colony's survival—that life has more meaning than a Master who lives eight hundred years in idle luxury."

[[CH05-P0400]] The words that had once brought tears of gratitude to her eyes now felt like chains, each phrase designed to make her accept something that should never have been accepted. She thought of Elena in the photographs, living to eighty-seven, dying in her garden surrounded by loved ones. She thought of her grandmother, dead at fifty-three from cellular breakdown that was now beginning to sound less like natural processes and more like planned termination.

[[CH05-P0410]] The service continued, but Marnette barely heard it. She went through the motions—standing when others stood, sitting when others sat, mouthing prayers she could no longer speak with conviction. When it finally ended, she felt hollow, scraped clean of something essential that she couldn't name.

[[CH05-P0420]] Lena caught her arm as they filed out into the corridor. "You didn't sing the closing hymn. Are you feeling alright?"

[[CH05-P0430]] "Just tired," Marnette repeated, the excuse becoming a shield. "The assignment took more out of me than I realized."

[[CH05-P0440]] "Of course it was draining. Caring for someone who's dying—really dying, not just aging—that takes something from you." Lena's concern was genuine, uncomplicated by the doubts that now plagued Marnette's every thought. "You should rest. Take a few days before accepting a new assignment. The caregiver coordinator will understand. After caring for a dying Master, you're entitled to recovery time."

[[CH05-P0450]] Recovery. As if what she was experiencing could be healed by rest, as if the fractures in her worldview could be mended by a few days of sleep and prayer.

[[CH05-P0460]] "I'll do that," Marnette said. "Thank you, Lena. For being here."

[[CH05-P0470]] They parted at the corridor junction, Lena heading toward the hydroponics district for her evening shift, Marnette walking slowly back toward her quarters. The flat lighting seemed to press down on her, the gray walls closing in. She had walked these corridors thousands of times, had always found them comforting in their familiarity. Now they felt like a cage she was only beginning to recognize.

[[CH05-P0480]] \*\*\*

[[CH05-P0490]] The days blurred together, each one a struggle to maintain the appearance of normalcy. Marnette attended services, visited with friends, went through the motions of a life that no longer felt like her own. She applied for recovery leave from the caregiver coordinator, citing exhaustion from the Frost assignment. The coordinator, a middle-aged woman named Vera who had processed hundreds of such requests, approved it without question—caring for a dying Master was understood to be emotionally demanding, and Marnette's record was impeccable.

[[CH05-P0500]] But the truth was that she couldn't imagine caring for anyone else right now. Couldn't imagine sitting beside another dying person, offering comfort she no longer believed in, speaking prayers that had become hollow incantations. The faith that had sustained her through eight years of watching people die had cracked, and she didn't know how to repair it.

[[CH05-P0510]] She tried to fill her days with activity. She volunteered in the community gardens, turning soil and planting seeds with mechanical precision. She visited elderly neighbors, bringing them meals and sitting with them through lonely evenings. She did everything she could to avoid being alone with her thoughts, with the questions that multiplied whenever silence gave them room to grow.

[[CH05-P0520]] Two weeks after her return, she met Lena for the midday meal in the communal dining hall. The space was crowded with salves from various work sectors—agricultural workers with dirt under their nails, maintenance crews in stained coveralls, caregivers in the same gray tunics Marnette wore. The conversation at nearby tables was the usual mix of work complaints, family news, and speculation about the year-long funeral proceedings.

[[CH05-P0530]] "Thomas has been asking about you," Lena said, pushing nutrient paste around her plate. Her expression was troubled in a way Marnette hadn't seen before. "He heard you were back and wanted to know if you'd talk to him."

[[CH05-P0540]] Marnette remembered Thomas—Lena's younger brother, nineteen years old, assigned to maintenance in the industrial sector. The one who had been asking uncomfortable questions about lifespan variations, who had been talking to older workers about pre-Optimization records.

[[CH05-P0550]] "What does he want to talk about?"

[[CH05-P0560]] Lena hesitated, lowering her voice and glancing around to make sure no one was listening. "He's gotten worse since you left. More questions, more doubts. He keeps asking why some salves die in their forties while others make it to sixty. Keeps saying it doesn't make sense if we're all designed by Elom for the same purpose."

[[CH05-P0570]] The irony was almost unbearable. Thomas was asking the same questions Marnette now carried, but from a place of naive curiosity rather than devastating knowledge. He sensed something wrong without knowing how deep the wrongness went.

[[CH05-P0580]] "Has he talked to anyone else about this?" Marnette asked carefully.

[[CH05-P0590]] "That's what worries me. He's been spending time with some of the older maintenance workers. The ones who remember before the Great Optimization twenty years ago. They tell him stories about how things used to be different, how the variations in lifespan were wider then, how the Church changed some of its teachings." Lena's voice dropped even lower. "He thinks they're hiding something. He thinks the whole system is built on lies."

[[CH05-P0600]] Marnette felt her chest tighten. "What do you want me to say to him?"

[[CH05-P0610]] "I don't know. You're good with people, good with difficult conversations. You've sat with dying people and helped them find peace. Maybe you can help him find peace too, help him accept what he can't change." Lena's eyes were worried, the green stains on her fingers seeming darker against her pale skin. "I'm scared for him, Marnette. Doubt is dangerous. It spreads, it isolates, it can destroy a person's place in the community. I've seen it happen."

[[CH05-P0620]] Marnette thought of her own isolation, of the growing distance between herself and everyone around her. She went through the same motions, said the same things, but she was no longer the same person. And she didn't know if that person could ever come back.

[[CH05-P0630]] "I'll talk to him," she said. "But I can't promise anything."

[[CH05-P0640]] "Thank you." Lena's relief was visible, her shoulders relaxing slightly. "He respects you, you know. He remembers when you helped grandmother at the end. He said you were the only one who really listened to her."

[[CH05-P0650]] The memory of Thomas as a boy, sitting in the corner of his grandmother's room while Marnette administered comfort care, surfaced unbidden. He had watched everything, asked questions about the medications, wanted to understand how things worked. Even then, he had been curious. Even then, he had wanted to know why.

[[CH05-P0660]] \*\*\*

[[CH05-P0670]] That evening, Marnette found Thomas in the maintenance break room, alone at a table in the corner with a cup of cold tea in front of him. He was taller than she remembered, his features sharper, the softness of adolescence giving way to the lean hardness of a young man who worked with his hands. But his eyes were the same—dark and searching, looking at the world as if trying to see through its surface to something hidden beneath.

[[CH05-P0680]] "Lena said you wanted to talk to me," Marnette said, sliding into the seat across from him.

[[CH05-P0690]] Thomas looked up, surprise flickering across his face before it settled into something more guarded. "I didn't think you'd actually come."

[[CH05-P0700]] "Why not?"

[[CH05-P0710]] "Because everyone else avoids me now. They think I'm dangerous. A doubter." He said the word like it was a diagnosis, a condition that set him apart from healthy people. "Lena told you, didn't she? About the questions I've been asking?"

[[CH05-P0720]] "She's worried about you."

[[CH05-P0730]] "She's worried about what people will think of her if I keep talking." There was bitterness in his voice, the sharp edge of someone who had been dismissed too many times. "But that's not why I wanted to talk to you. I wanted to ask about Master Frost."

[[CH05-P0740]] Marnette felt her pulse quicken. "What about him?"

[[CH05-P0750]] "They say he was one of the oldest. One of the ones who remembered the beginning." Thomas leaned forward, his voice dropping. "Did he ever say anything about how things used to be? About what the founders intended? About why things are the way they are?"

[[CH05-P0760]] The question hit too close to the truth, and Marnette found herself struggling to respond. She could lie, dismiss his questions the way everyone else had, send him back to the comfort of accepted beliefs. Or she could tell him something closer to the truth and risk everything—for both of them.

[[CH05-P0770]] "He said a lot of things," she finally answered. "Dying people often do. Their minds wander to the past, to regrets, to things they wish they had done differently."

[[CH05-P0780]] "But did he say anything specific? About the lifespans? About why we only live fifty or sixty years while they live for centuries?"

[[CH05-P0790]] Marnette looked at Thomas's young face, at the desperate hunger for understanding that burned in his eyes. He was nineteen—probably thirty-five or forty years from his own death, depending on the variables no one fully understood. Thirty-five or forty years to live with questions that might never be answered, or with answers that might destroy him.

[[CH05-P0800]] "Thomas," she said carefully, "sometimes the search for truth is more dangerous than the ignorance we leave behind. Are you sure you want to find answers?"

[[CH05-P0810]] His expression hardened. "I'm sure I can't live without looking. I'm sure that pretending everything is fine when nothing makes sense is its own kind of death."

[[CH05-P0820]] The words could have been her own. The determination in his eyes mirrored something she felt in her own chest, something that had been growing since Frost's final words had lodged themselves in her mind like splinters.

[[CH05-P0830]] "Then keep looking," she said quietly. "But be careful. And don't trust anyone who gives you easy answers."

[[CH05-P0840]] It wasn't the comfort Lena had wanted her to provide. But it was the only honest thing she could offer.

[[CH05-P0850]] \*\*\*

[[CH05-P0860]] The nights were the hardest. Lying in her small room, staring at the ceiling, Marnette's mind replayed Frost's final words on an endless loop. Archive... find truth... they lied... Elon not Elom... forgive... The fragments assembled themselves into accusations, into commands, into a promise she had made and couldn't escape.

[[CH05-P0870]] She had promised a dying man that she would look. That she would go to the Archive and find whatever truth was hidden there. It had seemed like such a small thing at the time—a way to comfort him in his final moments, a gesture of respect for a man who was carrying unbearable guilt. But now the promise weighed on her like a physical burden, a debt that grew heavier with each passing day.

[[CH05-P0880]] Three weeks after her return, she tried to pray. Really pray, the way she had before the assignment, with genuine faith and open heart. She knelt by her bed in the darkness, her prayer beads cool against her palms, and began the words she had spoken thousands of times.

[[CH05-P0890]] "Elom, creator and guide..."

[[CH05-P0900]] The words stopped there. She couldn't continue. Every phrase that followed had been corrupted by knowledge, twisted by doubt until it meant something entirely different from what she had always believed.

[[CH05-P0910]] Who designed us for purpose—engineers, not divine creator.

[[CH05-P0920]] Who gave each of us our role—Masters voting to create biological castes.

[[CH05-P0930]] To find joy in our designated time—to accept without question a death sentence encoded in their very cells.

[[CH05-P0940]] Marnette bowed her head, but no prayer came. Instead, she found herself speaking to Frost, to the memory of him, to the ghost of a man who had lived nearly eight centuries and died trying to undo a fraction of the harm he had helped cause.

[[CH05-P0950]] "I promised," she whispered into the darkness. "I promised I would look. But how? The Archive is in your residence, and your residence is Master property. I can't just walk in and demand access to records that would undermine everything the colony is built on."

[[CH05-P0960]] No answer came, of course. The dead didn't speak, no matter how much you wanted them to. But the question remained, demanding an answer she didn't have.

[[CH05-P0970]] \*\*\*

[[CH05-P0980]] The funeral proceedings for Master Frost continued throughout the colony, elaborate ceremonies that would last the full year of mourning. Public displays were erected in the common areas, showing images from his long life—Frost as a young man in the early colony days, Frost serving on various councils, Frost receiving honors for his historical scholarship. The images showed someone confident, powerful, certain of his place in the world. They didn't show the man Marnette had known—frail, dying, desperate to confess before his body finally gave out.

[[CH05-P0990]] One month after his death, Marnette walked past one of these displays and stopped. The image showed Frost standing in his Archive, surrounded by his collection of books and data crystals, looking like a scholar in his element. The caption read: "Master Frost's personal archive contained the most comprehensive collection of early colony records in existence. His scholarly contributions to our understanding of the founding period were invaluable."

[[CH05-P1000]] His personal archive. The most comprehensive collection of early colony records in existence.

[[CH05-P1010]] And she had been inside it. She had been granted access, her clearance programmed by Frost himself. She had seen the book with Project Mayfly, had glimpsed the photographs of Elena living to eighty-seven, had touched the leather bindings of documents that contained truth the colony had spent centuries hiding.

[[CH05-P1020]] The question wasn't whether the truth was there. She knew it was. The question was whether she could reach it.

[[CH05-P1030]] \*\*\*

[[CH05-P1040]] That night, Marnette lay awake thinking about Residence 7B. After a Master's death, their residence would eventually be reassigned—cleaned, renovated, prepared for a new occupant. But that process took time, especially for someone as prominent as Frost. His collections would need to be catalogued, his affairs settled, his vast accumulation of centuries sorted and disposed of. During the year-long mourning period, the residence would likely remain essentially untouched, a shrine to the deceased.

[[CH05-P1050]] Which meant the Archive would still be there. The books, the data crystals, the photographs, the evidence of everything Frost had tried to tell her—all of it waiting in that room she had entered once before.

[[CH05-P1060]] But how could she access it? She was a salve, a caregiver whose assignment had ended with Frost's death. She had no legitimate reason to return to his residence, no authority to enter Master property, no cover story that would explain her presence in a place she no longer belonged.

[[CH05-P1070]] Unless.

[[CH05-P1080]] The thought came slowly, shamefully, but it wouldn't leave her alone. Elara was still there. Elara, the household manager who had served Frost for decades, who had warned Marnette to be careful with her observations, who had seemed to understand more than she ever said. Elara, who might still have access to the residence during the transition period.

[[CH05-P1090]] Elara, who might be convinced to help.

[[CH05-P1100]] Master Frost’s room had come apart in seconds. The monitor above his bed shrieked, red strobes cutting across displays that should have been steady green, and the emergency lighting turned everything thin and unreal. Frost thrashed against the sheets, his spare frame bowing and twisting as if the bed were trying to hold down a storm. His hands clawed at the air, grasping for something Marnette could not see, and the sound that had pulled her from sleep seemed to still be trapped in the room, sharp as pain. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1110]] She needed help. Elara was the only person she knew who might understand why. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1120]] *** <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1130]] Over the next few days the thought stopped being a possibility and became an intention, then a plan. Marnette would contact Elara. She would find a way back to Residence 7B. She would get into the Archive and find what Frost had pointed her towards, whatever it cost her to see it clearly. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1140]] What came after remained unclear, and the uncertainty frightened her more than the plan itself. If she found proof that salve lifespans were engineered, that the entire social order rested on biological manipulation and religious lies, what would she do with it. Who could she tell. How could she make anyone believe her. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1150]] Tell them, Frost had whispered at the end. Marnette still did not know who he meant. Other salves, perhaps. Yet would they even want the truth if it stripped away the one story that made their lives bearable. Would they believe her, or close their hands over their ears and call her dangerous. Would they respond like Lena, all warmth and worry, and then a quiet insistence that she rest and return to sense. Or like Thomas, eyes bright with need, ready to reach for an answer no matter what it burned. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1160]] The questions had no clean answers, but the first step was plain. She had to know. She had to see for herself what Frost’s Archive contained, not in fragments and half-glimpses but whole. Whatever came after would have to be built on that. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1170]] Six weeks after Frost’s death, Marnette woke before dawn with a clarity she had not felt since leaving his residence. Grey artificial morning leaked through the small window, revealing a room she had lived in for years and no longer recognised as home. Her prayer beads sat untouched on the bedside table. The carving of Elom faced her, familiar in shape and suddenly strange, its gaze no longer a comfort she could rely on. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1180]] She rose, pulled on her grey tunic, and studied herself in the small mirror by the door. The face looking back had not changed. Same dark eyes, same strong features, the same practiced calm that had carried her into Frost’s residence. The difference lay underneath, in the way her gaze held without softening. Something had shifted behind it, a hairline break that had also become an opening. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1190]] "Today," she said to her reflection. "I’ll find a way." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1200]] She did not know how. She did not know what obstacles waited, or what danger a single misstep might summon. She only knew she could not keep living like this. She could not keep performing faith that no longer held. She could not keep shaping her mouth around prayers that tasted false, then call it devotion. Frost’s promise sat in her like a stone. If she did not try, she would be breaking it every day. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1210]] Frost’s Archive waited in Residence 7B, holding secrets that could unmake the world she knew. Marnette Croft had spent her life practising acceptance until it felt like virtue. Now she was ready to ask what she had been trained never to ask, even if the questions tore her apart. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1220]] She took her prayer beads from the bedside table and let their familiar weight settle into her palm. For a moment she considered leaving them behind, a clean gesture, an easy symbol. The thought felt dishonest. Instead she tucked them beneath her collar, where they had always rested. They were part of her, whatever she believed now, and she could not cut them away without cutting herself. Perhaps the truth, when she found it, would tell her what the beads were for. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1230]] The corridor outside her quarters lay empty in the early light, flat and shadowless. Marnette headed for the transit platform with her steps kept even, as if steadiness were something she could choose and hold. Her mind had stopped circling and narrowed into purpose. She would get back to Residence 7B. She would get into the Archive. She would uncover whatever Frost had been trying to place in her hands. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1240]] Whatever followed, whatever consequences came with knowing what had been buried for centuries, she would meet them. The alternative was to spend the rest of her designated time performing ignorance, pretending the cracks in her faith were not there, then die without ever understanding why. She could not accept that any longer. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1250]] The cabin’s vibration rose under her feet as Marnette stepped aboard and took her seat, hands clasped to keep them from shaking. The route ahead was familiar. What it now meant was not. Behind her, the salve district kept to its morning routines, corridors filling, shifts beginning, prayers spoken on time, as if certainty were still available. Marnette stared at the closed doors and felt the threshold settle into place. The questions waiting for her had no safe answers, and the truths behind them would not be gentle. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1260]] Marnette watched the grey corridors fall away into the transit tubes, her familiar world sliding past the grimy window in clipped, repeating segments. Something moved under her ribs, too sharp to name cleanly. It might have been fear. It might have been hope. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1270]] Frost’s last words returned, stripped down to their bones. The Archive. Find the truth. They lied. The founder’s name, not the one she had been taught. Forgive. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1280]] She leaned closer to the window and whispered to the empty car, as if the promise could travel ahead of her. "I’m coming. I’ll find it. I promise." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1290]] Red dust lay beyond the dome, patient as ever, indifferent to vows and mourning. Somewhere in Residence 7B, behind polished floors and locked doors, a room full of records waited in silence. Frost had called it an Archive. Marnette felt it ahead of her like gravity, dangerous and unavoidable, asking to be opened. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH05-P1300]] Marnette Croft was finally ready to look. <!-- REV2 -->
